title: CLAUDE.md — Session context for ggmemo
text: ggmemo R package adds callout change annotations ggplot2 business charts. Two exported functions: annotate_callout() annotate_change(). Built ggpp, uses tidy eval row selection, dplyr dependency. Target audience: business analysts making reports presentations ggplot2.

title: CLAUDE.md — Session context for ggmemo
text: files contain binding design decisions project context. Read making changes: notes/DESIGN.md — design decisions, known limitations notes/v0.2-prompt.md — detailed plan next release NEWS.md — ’s shipped README.md — user-facing pitch examples

title: CLAUDE.md — Session context for ggmemo
text: Use ggplot2:: ggpp:: namespace prefixes package code. @importFrom except rlang::.env. One exported function per R/annotate-*.R file. Shared internal helpers go R/utils.R (general) R/utils-change.R (change-specific). Error messages: stop(..., call. = FALSE), clear actionable, fix suggestions common user mistakes. comments explaining code . comment ’s non-obvious. Factor x-axes examples: always specify levels avoid alphabetical sorting.

title: CLAUDE.md — Session context for ggmemo
text: Use devtools:: usethis:: package development tasks. Run devtools::check() every change — target zero errors, zero warnings. Don’t add dependencies without asking. Write code asked. design decisions, propose options let choose. Defer unrelated ideas GitHub issues rather expanding current task.

title: CLAUDE.md — Session context for ggmemo
text: Small, descriptive commits (one logical change per commit). End commit messages Co-Authored-: Claude <noreply@anthropic.com>. Never amend published commits. Reference issue numbers Closes #N work completes issue.

title: CLAUDE.md — Session context for ggmemo
text: test-X.R corresponds R/X.R. Use vdiffr snapshots visual output tests. Run testthat::snapshot_accept("test-name") intentional visual changes. Test pure logic helpers separately plotting (faster, diagnostic).

title: CLAUDE.md — Session context for ggmemo
text: idea unrelated current task, ’ll say “open issue X.” Capture via gh issue create appropriate labels, return current task. give GitHub issue URL say “work ,” fetch issue body (gh issue view <number>), confirm scope , proceed. closing work tied issue, reference issue number commit message (Closes #12).

title: CLAUDE.md — Session context for ggmemo
code: R/                      Package source   annotate-callout.R      annotate_callout()   annotate-change.R       annotate_change()   utils.R                 Shared helpers (filter, nudge, detect x col)   utils-change.R          Change-specific helpers (delta, format, validate)   ggmemo-package.R        Package-level docs tests/testthat/         Unit tests and vdiffr snapshots vignettes/              "Narrating Business Charts with ggmemo" notes/                  Design docs, code walkthrough, v0.2 prompt (not installed with package) man/                    Generated .Rd help files (don't edit directly) .github/workflows/      R-CMD-check, test-coverage, pkgdown

title: CLAUDE.md — Session context for ggmemo
code: devtools::check()          # Full R CMD check devtools::test()           # Run testthat suite devtools::document()       # Regenerate NAMESPACE and .Rd from roxygen devtools::build_readme()   # Knit README.Rmd -> README.md pkgdown::build_site()      # Build pkgdown site locally in docs/

title: Contributor Covenant Code of Conduct
text: members, contributors, leaders pledge make participation community harassment-free experience everyone, regardless age, body size, visible invisible disability, ethnicity, sex characteristics, gender identity expression, level experience, education, socio-economic status, nationality, personal appearance, race, caste, color, religion, sexual identity orientation. pledge act interact ways contribute open, welcoming, diverse, inclusive, healthy community.

title: Contributor Covenant Code of Conduct
text: Examples behavior contributes positive environment community include: Demonstrating empathy kindness toward people respectful differing opinions, viewpoints, experiences Giving gracefully accepting constructive feedback Accepting responsibility apologizing affected mistakes, learning experience Focusing best just us individuals, overall community Examples unacceptable behavior include: use sexualized language imagery, sexual attention advances kind Trolling, insulting derogatory comments, personal political attacks Public private harassment Publishing others’ private information, physical email address, without explicit permission conduct reasonably considered inappropriate professional setting

title: Contributor Covenant Code of Conduct
text: Community leaders responsible clarifying enforcing standards acceptable behavior take appropriate fair corrective action response behavior deem inappropriate, threatening, offensive, harmful. Community leaders right responsibility remove, edit, reject comments, commits, code, wiki edits, issues, contributions aligned Code Conduct, communicate reasons moderation decisions appropriate.

title: Contributing to ggmemo
text: outlines propose change ggmemo. detailed discussion contributing tidyverse packages, please see development contributing guide code review principles.

title: Contributing to ggmemo
text: can fix typos, spelling mistakes, grammatical errors documentation directly using GitHub web interface, long changes made source file. generally means ’ll need edit roxygen2 comments .R, .Rd file. can find .R file generates .Rd reading comment first line.

title: Contributing to ggmemo
text: want make bigger change, ’s good idea first file issue make sure someone team agrees ’s needed. ’ve found bug, please file issue illustrates bug minimal reprex (also help write unit test, needed). See guide create great issue advice.

title: Contributing to ggmemo
text: Fork package clone onto computer. haven’t done , recommend using usethis::create_from_github("lindsay-lintelman/ggmemo", fork = TRUE). Install development dependencies devtools::install_dev_deps(), make sure package passes R CMD check running devtools::check(). R CMD check doesn’t pass cleanly, ’s good idea ask help continuing. Create Git branch pull request (PR). recommend using usethis::pr_init("brief-description--change"). Make changes, commit git, create PR running usethis::pr_push(), following prompts browser. title PR briefly describe change. body PR contain Fixes #issue-number. user-facing changes, add bullet top NEWS.md (.e. just first header). Follow style described https://style.tidyverse.org/news.html.

title: Contributing to ggmemo
text: New code follow tidyverse style guide. can use Air apply style, please don’t restyle code nothing PR. use roxygen2, Markdown syntax, documentation. use testthat unit tests. Contributions test cases included easier accept.

title: Contributing to ggmemo

title: Narrating Business Charts with ggmemo
code: library(ggplot2) library(ggmemo)  revenue <- data.frame(   quarter = factor(c("Q1", "Q2", "Q3", "Q4"),                    levels = c("Q1", "Q2", "Q3", "Q4")),   revenue = c(120, 145, 132, 158) )

title: Narrating Business Charts with ggmemo
text: bar chart accurate, doesn’t guide reader. quarter matters? trend good bad? viewer figure .
code: p <- ggplot(revenue, aes(x = quarter, y = revenue)) +   geom_col(fill = "steelblue", width = 0.6) +   labs(title = "2024 Quarterly Revenue ($K)", x = NULL, y = NULL) +   theme_minimal() p

what: Calling out a key data point
title: Narrating Business Charts with ggmemo
text: annotate_callout() points specific row data arrow label. identify row filter expression — syntax use dplyr::filter():  position argument controls label sits relative data point. Options "top-right" (default), "top-left", "bottom-right", "bottom-left".
code: p +   annotate_callout(     revenue,     where = quarter == "Q4",     label = "Record quarter",     position = "top-left"   ) #> Registered S3 methods overwritten by 'ggpp': #>   method                  from    #>   heightDetails.titleGrob ggplot2 #>   widthDetails.titleGrob  ggplot2

what: Showing the change between two points
title: Narrating Business Charts with ggmemo
text: annotate_change() draws color-coded arrow two rows labels midpoint computed delta — green increases, red decreases:
code: p +   annotate_change(     revenue,     from = quarter == "Q1",     to = quarter == "Q4",     value = revenue   )

previous_headings: Showing the change between two points
title: Narrating Business Charts with ggmemo
text: format argument controls delta displayed. default "percent". options: Absolute difference — shows raw numeric change:  Percentage points — useful data already expressed rate percentage (e.g., savings rate, market share). Using "percent" rate data gives misleading percent--percent; "points" gives straightforward difference:
code: p +   annotate_change(     revenue,     from = quarter == "Q1",     to = quarter == "Q4",     value = revenue,     format = "absolute"   ) rates <- data.frame(   year = 2020:2023,   rate = c(3.5, 8.1, 5.4, 3.7) )  ggplot(rates, aes(x = year, y = rate)) +   geom_line() +   geom_point() +   annotate_change(     rates,     from = year == 2020,     to = year == 2021,     value = rate,     format = "points"   ) +   labs(title = "Unemployment Rate", y = "Rate (%)") +   theme_minimal()

title: Narrating Business Charts with ggmemo
text: can combine functions one chart. callout names moment; change annotation quantifies happened:
code: ggplot(revenue, aes(x = quarter, y = revenue)) +   geom_col(fill = "steelblue", width = 0.6) +   annotate_callout(     revenue,     where = quarter == "Q4",     label = "Record quarter",     position = "top-left"   ) +   annotate_change(     revenue,     from = quarter == "Q1",     to = quarter == "Q4",     value = revenue   ) +   labs(title = "2024 Quarterly Revenue ($K)", x = NULL, y = NULL) +   theme_minimal()

title: Narrating Business Charts with ggmemo
text: can stack several annotate_change() calls show quarter--quarter movement across full series:
code: ggplot(revenue, aes(x = quarter, y = revenue)) +   geom_col(fill = "grey70", width = 0.6) +   annotate_change(revenue, from = quarter == "Q1",                   to = quarter == "Q2", value = revenue) +   annotate_change(revenue, from = quarter == "Q2",                   to = quarter == "Q3", value = revenue) +   annotate_change(revenue, from = quarter == "Q3",                   to = quarter == "Q4", value = revenue) +   labs(title = "Quarter-over-Quarter Changes", x = NULL, y = NULL) +   theme_minimal() #> Coordinate system already present. #> ℹ Adding new coordinate system, which will replace the existing one. #> Scale for y is already present. #> Adding another scale for y, which will replace the existing scale. #> Coordinate system already present. #> ℹ Adding new coordinate system, which will replace the existing one. #> Scale for y is already present. #> Adding another scale for y, which will replace the existing scale.

title: Narrating Business Charts with ggmemo
text: ggmemo works Date x-axes. ’s savings rate time series callout -time low change annotation showing recovery. Note use nudge manually position callout label — overrides automatic heuristic, can miss wide data frames many numeric columns (see Nudge ):
code: ggplot(economics, aes(x = date, y = psavert)) +   geom_line(colour = "grey40") +   annotate_callout(     economics,     where = date == as.Date("2005-07-01"),     label = "All-time low",     nudge = c(365, 1)   ) +   annotate_change(     economics,     from = date == as.Date("2005-07-01"),     to = date == as.Date("2012-12-01"),     value = psavert,     format = "points"   ) +   labs(     title = "U.S. Personal Savings Rate",     subtitle = "Recovery after the 2005 low",     x = NULL, y = "Savings rate (%)"   ) +   theme_minimal()

title: Narrating Business Charts with ggmemo
text: annotate_change() uses dark green increases, dark red decreases, grey change default. can supply palette colors argument — named vector entries , , flat:
code: p +   annotate_change(     revenue,     from = quarter == "Q1",     to = quarter == "Q4",     value = revenue,     colors = c(up = "#1B9E77", down = "#D95F02", flat = "#7570B3")   )

title: Narrating Business Charts with ggmemo
text: functions accept ..., passes additional arguments underlying ggplot2 layer. Use override defaults like text size, background fill, text colour:
code: p +   annotate_callout(     revenue,     where = quarter == "Q4",     label = "Record quarter",     position = "top-left",     size = 5,     fill = "lightyellow",     colour = "grey30"   )

title: Narrating Business Charts with ggmemo
text: annotate_callout() automatically computes far offset label data point based data ranges. works well simple two-column data frames. wider data frames many numeric columns (like ggplot2::economics), heuristic may pick wrong column’s range produce label ’s far away close. can override heuristic passing nudge = c(x, y) data units:  Alternatively, can pass two-column subset data heuristic less guess:
code: ggplot(economics, aes(x = date, y = unemploy)) +   geom_line() +   annotate_callout(     economics,     where = date == as.Date("2009-10-01"),     label = "Peak unemployment",     nudge = c(800, 1000)   ) +   theme_minimal() annotate_callout(   economics[, c("date", "unemploy")],   where = date == as.Date("2009-10-01"),   label = "Peak unemployment" )

title: Narrating Business Charts with ggmemo
text: common issues come getting started ggmemo.

title: Narrating Business Charts with ggmemo
text: x-axis column character vector (common read.csv()), annotate_change() error suggest converting . , always specify levels preserve order data — plain factor() sorts alphabetically:
code: # Alphabetical — probably not what you want data$month <- factor(data$month)  # Preserves data order data$month <- factor(data$month, levels = unique(data$month))

title: Narrating Business Charts with ggmemo
text: CSV files store dates strings. date column looks like "2024-01-15" class character, convert plotting: ggmemo detect date-like strings suggest error message.
code: data$date <- as.Date(data$date)

title: Narrating Business Charts with ggmemo
text: ggplot2 uses British spelling internally. American color works contexts, can produce “Duplicated aesthetics” warning function already sets default colour. Using colour avoids warning:
code: annotate_callout(..., colour = "red")

what: size controls text, not the label box
title: Narrating Business Charts with ggmemo
text: size argument sets text size (mm, matching ggplot2 conventions). adjust padding around text inside label box, use label.padding:
code: annotate_callout(..., size = 5, label.padding = unit(0.4, "lines"))

what: Real-world example: NBA Finals scoring breakdown
title: Narrating Business Charts with ggmemo
text: examples use simple, two-column data. ’s complex chart — grouped stacked bar chart quarter--quarter scoring NBA Finals game — shows annotate_change() works annotation data differs plot data. Brunson scored 52% Knicks’ fourth-quarter points contributing just 20% Q3. annotate shift, pass separate two-row data frame annotate_change() x positions values want compare, use custom label, set expand_y = FALSE arrow doesn’t push y-axis beyond bars. also override default green orange used Brunson’s bars annotation feels integrated:  Key techniques used : Separate annotation data — plot uses full scoring data frame, annotate_change() receives minimal two-row frame just x positions values compare. Custom label — instead auto-computed delta, pass descriptive label tells story directly. expand_y = FALSE — prevents arrow adding extra space bars. Custom colors — colors = c(= "#E86A00", ...) matches arrow Brunson’s bar color annotation looks intentional.
code: scoring <- data.frame(   Quarter = rep(c("Q1", "Q2", "Q3", "Q4"), 4),   Team = rep(c("Knicks", "Knicks", "Spurs", "Spurs"), each = 4),   Player = rep(c("Brunson", "Rest of Knicks", "Wemby", "Rest of Spurs"), each = 4),   Points = c(     5, 7, 5, 13,     23, 20, 20, 12,     8, 9, 7, 4,     16, 17, 19, 15   ) )  scoring$Quarter <- factor(scoring$Quarter, levels = c("Q1", "Q2", "Q3", "Q4")) scoring$Player <- factor(scoring$Player,   levels = c("Rest of Spurs", "Wemby", "Rest of Knicks", "Brunson"))  scoring$x_pos <- as.numeric(scoring$Quarter) +   ifelse(scoring$Team == "Knicks", -0.2, 0.2)  team_totals <- aggregate(Points ~ Quarter + Team + x_pos, data = scoring, FUN = sum) team_totals <- team_totals[!duplicated(team_totals[, c("Quarter", "Team")]), ]  ggplot(scoring, aes(x = x_pos, y = Points, fill = Player)) +   geom_col(width = 0.35) +   geom_text(     data = team_totals,     aes(x = x_pos, y = Points, label = Points, fill = NULL),     vjust = -0.4, size = 5, fontface = "bold"   ) +   scale_fill_manual(     values = c(       "Brunson" = "#E86A00",       "Rest of Knicks" = "#FDCB8B",       "Wemby" = "#6D6D6D",       "Rest of Spurs" = "#B8B8B8"     ),     name = NULL   ) +   scale_x_continuous(breaks = 1:4, labels = c("Q1", "Q2", "Q3", "Q4")) +   scale_y_continuous(expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.12))) +   annotate_change(     data.frame(x_pos = c(2.8, 3.8), Points = c(5, 13)),     from = x_pos == 2.8,     to = x_pos == 3.8,     value = Points,     format = "percent",     expand_y = FALSE,     label = "20% → 52% of team pts",     colors = c(up = "#E86A00", down = "#B22222", flat = "#808080")   ) +   coord_cartesian(clip = "off") +   labs(     title = "Quarter-by-Quarter Scoring — Knicks vs Spurs",     x = NULL, y = "Points"   ) +   theme_minimal(base_size = 14) +   theme(     legend.position = "bottom",     panel.grid.major.x = element_blank(),     plot.margin = margin(10, 20, 10, 10)   ) #> Coordinate system already present. #> ℹ Adding new coordinate system, which will replace the existing one.

title: Narrating Business Charts with ggmemo
text: ggmemo focused two tasks: callout annotations change annotations business charts. annotation needs, packages worth knowing: Label overlap avoidance: ggrepel automatically repositions text labels avoid overlaps. Precise NPC positioning: ggpp supports normalized parent coordinates package ggmemo builds . Interactive annotations: plotly ggiraph support hover tooltips, click events, zoom. Themes styling: ggthemes, hrbrthemes, bbplot provide polished chart themes business publication contexts.

what: ggmemo: Add Arrows, Labels, and Change Annotations to ggplot2 Charts
title: ggmemo: Add Arrows, Labels, and Change Annotations to ggplot2 Charts
text: Add arrows, labels, change annotations ggplot2 business charts one line code.

title: ggmemo: Add Arrows, Labels, and Change Annotations to ggplot2 Charts
text: Without ggmemo, annotating ggplot2 chart means hardcoding coordinates, computing deltas, formatting labels, choosing colors — roughly 10 lines manual work per annotation. ggmemo replaces single function call.

title: ggmemo: Add Arrows, Labels, and Change Annotations to ggplot2 Charts
code: # install.packages("pak") pak::pak("lindsay-lintelman/ggmemo")

title: ggmemo: Add Arrows, Labels, and Change Annotations to ggplot2 Charts
text: Charts rendered 7 × 4.5 , 150 dpi.

title: ggmemo: Add Arrows, Labels, and Change Annotations to ggplot2 Charts
text: Point specific data row arrow label:
code: library(ggplot2) library(ggmemo)  # fig.width = 7, fig.height = 4.5, dpi = 150 econ <- economics econ$unemp_rate <- econ$unemploy / econ$pop * 100  ggplot(econ, aes(x = date, y = unemp_rate)) +   geom_line() +   annotate_callout(     econ,     where = date == as.Date("1982-12-01"),     label = "Volcker recession",     position = "top-right",     nudge = c(-1500, 0.5),     arrow = NULL,     point.padding = 2,     alpha = .7   ) +   annotate_callout(     econ,     where = date == as.Date("2009-10-01"),     label = "'08 Financial Crisis",     position = "bottom-right",     nudge = c(-2500, 0.5),     arrow = NULL,     point.padding = 2,     alpha = .7   ) +   annotate_change(     econ,     from = date == as.Date("1982-12-01"),     to = date == as.Date("2009-10-01"),     value = unemp_rate,     arrow_size = .08,     arrow_type = "closed",     curvature = -.3,     format = "points"   ) +   scale_y_continuous(expand = expansion(mult = c(0.05, 0.15))) +   labs(title = "U.S. Unemployment as Share of Population (%)",        x = NULL, y = NULL) +   theme(plot.margin = margin(10, 60, 10, 10))

title: ggmemo: Add Arrows, Labels, and Change Annotations to ggplot2 Charts
text: Show delta two data points color-coded arrow:
code: # fig.width = 7, fig.height = 4.5, dpi = 150 econ <- economics econ$unemp_rate <- econ$unemploy / econ$pop * 100  ggplot(econ, aes(x = date, y = unemp_rate)) +   geom_line(colour = "grey40") +   annotate_change(     econ,     from = date == as.Date("2009-10-01"),     to = date == as.Date("2015-03-01"),     value = unemp_rate,     arrow_pad = 0.05,     curvature = -.35,     arrow_size = 0.08,     x = as.Date("2015-01-01"),     y = 4.95,     fill = NA   ) +   labs(title = "U.S. Unemployment as Share of Population (%)",        x = NULL, y = NULL) +   theme_minimal()

title: ggmemo: Add Arrows, Labels, and Change Annotations to ggplot2 Charts
code: # fig.width = 7, fig.height = 4.5, dpi = 150 quarterly_revenue <- data.frame(   quarter = factor(c("Q1", "Q2", "Q3", "Q4"),                    levels = c("Q1", "Q2", "Q3", "Q4")),   revenue = c(120, 145, 132, 158) )  ggplot(quarterly_revenue, aes(x = quarter, y = revenue)) +   geom_col(fill = "steelblue", width = 0.6) +   annotate_change(     quarterly_revenue,     from = quarter == "Q3",     to = quarter == "Q4",     value = revenue,     arrow_pad = .2,     curvature = -.3,     arrow_type = "closed",     y = 180,     fill = NA   ) +   scale_y_continuous(expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.4))) +   labs(title = "Quarterly Revenue ($K)", x = NULL, y = NULL) +   theme_minimal(base_size = 13)

title: ggmemo: Add Arrows, Labels, and Change Annotations to ggplot2 Charts
text: ggmemo focused callout change annotations business charts. annotation needs: ggpp — precise annotation positioning NPC coordinates. ggmemo built ggpp. ggrepel — automatically reposition text labels avoid overlaps. ggannotate — interactively annotate plots RStudio.

what: Add a callout annotation to a ggplot — annotate_callout
title: Add a callout annotation to a ggplot — annotate_callout
text: Points specific data row arrow label. callout consists text label inside rounded box, connected target data point line segment arrowhead. Built top ggpp::geom_label_s().

title: Add a callout annotation to a ggplot — annotate_callout
code: annotate_callout(data, where, label, position = "top-right", nudge = NULL, ...)

title: Add a callout annotation to a ggplot — annotate_callout
text: data data frame. data frame used ggplot, subset . Must contain columns mapped x y plot's aes(). Note: automatic nudge heuristic estimates label offset data ranges, guesses columns x y. data frames many numeric columns, passing two-column subset (e.g., data[, c("date", "sales")]) setting nudge explicitly gives reliable placement. <tidy-eval> filtering expression identifies exactly one row data. example, year == 2020 quarter == "Q4" & region == "West". error thrown expression matches zero one row. label single character string annotation text. position place label relative data point. One "top-right" (default), "top-left", "bottom-right", "bottom-left". nudge Optional numeric vector length 2 (c(x, y)) giving explicit nudge amounts data units. Overrides automatic nudge heuristic, estimates 5% x y data ranges. heuristic works well data contains plotted columns; data many numeric columns (like ggplot2::economics), passing two-column subset setting nudge explicitly avoids heuristic picking wrong column's range. ... Additional arguments passed ggpp::geom_label_s(). Use override defaults like size, colour, fill, alpha, arrow.

title: Add a callout annotation to a ggplot — annotate_callout
text: ggplot2 layer can added plot +.

title: Add a callout annotation to a ggplot — annotate_callout
code: library(ggplot2)  p <- ggplot(economics, aes(x = date, y = unemploy)) +   geom_line()  # Basic callout p + annotate_callout(   economics,   where = date == as.Date("2009-10-01"),   label = "Peak unemployment",   position = "top-right" )   # With explicit nudge (useful when data has many numeric columns) p + annotate_callout(   economics,   where = date == as.Date("2009-10-01"),   label = "Peak unemployment",   nudge = c(365, 500) )   # Customize label appearance via ... (larger text, yellow background) p + annotate_callout(   economics,   where = date == as.Date("2009-10-01"),   label = "Peak unemployment",   nudge = c(365, 500),   size = 5, fill = "lightyellow" )   # Mark both the peak and the trough on the same chart p +   annotate_callout(     economics,     where = date == as.Date("2009-10-01"),     label = "Peak",     nudge = c(365, 500)   ) +   annotate_callout(     economics,     where = date == as.Date("2000-01-01"),     label = "Dot-com low",     position = "bottom-right",     nudge = c(365, 500)   )

what: Annotate the change between two data points on a ggplot — annotate_change
title: Annotate the change between two data points on a ggplot — annotate_change
text: Draws curved arrow two data rows labels midpoint computed delta. label color-coded: dark green increases, dark red decreases, grey change. Built top ggplot2::annotate().

title: Annotate the change between two data points on a ggplot — annotate_change
code: annotate_change(   data,   from,   to,   value,   format = "percent",   colors = c(up = "#2E7D32", down = "#B22222", flat = "#808080"),   curvature = -0.2,   arrow_pad = 0.04,   expand_y = TRUE,   ... )

title: Annotate the change between two data points on a ggplot — annotate_change
text: data data frame. data frame used ggplot. Must contain columns mapped x y plot's aes(), well column specified value. <tidy-eval> filtering expression identifies exactly one row data start arrow. example, quarter == "Q2". error thrown expression matches zero one row. <tidy-eval> filtering expression identifies exactly one row data end arrow. value <tidy-eval> unquoted column name indicating numeric column compute change . example, value = revenue. format format delta label. One "percent" (default), "absolute", "points", "". Percent change zero base value falls back absolute warning. Percent change negative values uses raw formula may confusing; use "absolute" data can go negative. Use "points" data already rate percentage (e.g., savings rate, market share) — labels difference percentage points (e.g., "+9.8 %pts") instead computing misleading percent--percent. colors Named character vector length 3 hex color values arrow label. Names must "", "", "flat". Defaults dark green, dark red, grey. curvature Numeric value controlling curve arrow. Positive values curve right, negative values curve left. Defaults -0.2 subtle leftward arc. Set 0 straight arrow. arrow_pad Fraction y-axis range lift arrow endpoints data values, creating visible whitespace arrow bars points. Defaults 0.04 (4%). Set 0 gap. expand_y Logical. TRUE (default) curvature non-zero, adds scale_y_continuous(expand = ...) layer prevent curved arrow clipped figure edge. expansion amount scales abs(curvature). Set FALSE suppress control y-axis expansion . ... Additional arguments passed label layer (ggplot2::annotate() geom = "label"). Use override defaults like size, fontface, fill. Note: affect arrow segment. change arrow, use colors.

title: Annotate the change between two data points on a ggplot — annotate_change
text: list ggplot2 layers (arrow, label, coord_cartesian(clip = ""), optionally scale_y_continuous(expand = ...)) can added plot +. coord layer prevents curved arrow clipped plot panel boundary; scale layer expands y-axis accommodate curve arc.

title: Annotate the change between two data points on a ggplot — annotate_change
text: curved arrow may arc outside default plot area. prevent clipping, function automatically includes coord_cartesian(clip = "") layer. need different coordinate system (e.g., coord_flip()), add annotate_change() takes precedence, set clip = "" coord keep arrow visible. expand_y = TRUE (default), function also adds scale_y_continuous(expand = ...) layer pads y-axis proportionally abs(curvature). set scale_y_continuous() annotate_change(), scale replaces one function.

title: Annotate the change between two data points on a ggplot — annotate_change
code: library(ggplot2)  revenue <- data.frame(   quarter = factor(c("Q1", "Q2", "Q3", "Q4"),                    levels = c("Q1", "Q2", "Q3", "Q4")),   revenue = c(120, 145, 132, 158) )  # Percent change (default) ggplot(revenue, aes(x = quarter, y = revenue)) +   geom_col(fill = "grey70", width = 0.6) +   annotate_change(     revenue,     from = quarter == "Q1",     to = quarter == "Q4",     value = revenue   )   # Absolute change ggplot(revenue, aes(x = quarter, y = revenue)) +   geom_col(fill = "grey70", width = 0.6) +   annotate_change(     revenue,     from = quarter == "Q1",     to = quarter == "Q4",     value = revenue,     format = "absolute"   )   # Percentage points (for data already expressed as rates) rates <- data.frame(   year = 2020:2023,   rate = c(3.5, 8.1, 5.4, 3.7) ) ggplot(rates, aes(x = year, y = rate)) +   geom_line() +   geom_point() +   annotate_change(rates, from = year == 2020, to = year == 2021,                   value = rate, format = "points")   # Custom colors (e.g., corporate palette) ggplot(revenue, aes(x = quarter, y = revenue)) +   geom_col(fill = "grey70", width = 0.6) +   annotate_change(     revenue,     from = quarter == "Q1",     to = quarter == "Q4",     value = revenue,     colors = c(up = "#1B9E77", down = "#D95F02", flat = "#7570B3")   )   # Date x-axis (time series) — use nudge on the callout for wide data ggplot(economics, aes(x = date, y = psavert)) +   geom_line() +   annotate_change(     economics,     from = date == as.Date("2005-07-01"),     to = date == as.Date("2012-12-01"),     value = psavert,     format = "points"   )   # Showing a decline (red arrow, negative label) ggplot(revenue, aes(x = quarter, y = revenue)) +   geom_col(fill = "grey70", width = 0.6) +   annotate_change(     revenue,     from = quarter == "Q2",     to = quarter == "Q3",     value = revenue   )   # Multiple change annotations (quarter-over-quarter) ggplot(revenue, aes(x = quarter, y = revenue)) +   geom_col(fill = "grey70", width = 0.6) +   annotate_change(revenue, from = quarter == "Q1",                   to = quarter == "Q2", value = revenue) +   annotate_change(revenue, from = quarter == "Q2",                   to = quarter == "Q3", value = revenue) +   annotate_change(revenue, from = quarter == "Q3",                   to = quarter == "Q4", value = revenue) #> Coordinate system already present. #> ℹ Adding new coordinate system, which will replace the existing one. #> Scale for y is already present. #> Adding another scale for y, which will replace the existing scale. #> Coordinate system already present. #> ℹ Adding new coordinate system, which will replace the existing one. #> Scale for y is already present. #> Adding another scale for y, which will replace the existing scale.   # Year-over-year growth on a line chart annual <- data.frame(year = 2019:2024,                      revenue = c(80, 65, 72, 95, 110, 128)) ggplot(annual, aes(x = year, y = revenue)) +   geom_line() + geom_point() +   annotate_change(annual, from = year == 2019,                   to = year == 2024, value = revenue) +   annotate_callout(annual, where = year == 2020,                    label = "COVID dip", position = "bottom-right")   # Combined with annotate_callout() on a time series ggplot(economics, aes(x = date, y = psavert)) +   geom_line() +   annotate_callout(     economics,     where = date == as.Date("2005-07-01"),     label = "All-time low",     nudge = c(365, 1)   ) +   annotate_change(     economics,     from = date == as.Date("2005-07-01"),     to = date == as.Date("2012-12-01"),     value = psavert,     format = "points"   )

what: ggmemo: Add Arrows, Labels, and Change Annotations to ggplot2 Charts — ggmemo-package
title: ggmemo: Add Arrows, Labels, and Change Annotations to ggplot2 Charts — ggmemo-package
text: Add arrows, labels, change annotations ggplot2 charts one line code. Two functions: annotate_callout(): Point data row arrow label. annotate_change(): Show delta two rows percent change, absolute change, percentage points. return standard ggplot2 layers — add +. Install GitHub (CRAN):
code: pak::pak("lindsay-lintelman/ggmemo")

what: Why ggmemo instead of manual ggplot2 annotation?
title: ggmemo: Add Arrows, Labels, and Change Annotations to ggplot2 Charts — ggmemo-package
text: Manual annotation requires hardcoding coordinates, computing deltas, formatting labels, picking colors (~10 lines). ggmemo replaces single function call:
code: # Without ggmemo: annotate("segment", x = "Q1", xend = "Q4", y = 120, yend = 158,          arrow = arrow(length = unit(0.15, "inches")),          colour = "#2E7D32", linewidth = 0.6) + annotate("label", x = 2.5, y = 139, label = "+31.7          colour = "#2E7D32", fill = "white", fontface = "bold")  # With ggmemo: annotate_change(data, from = quarter == "Q1",                 to = quarter == "Q4", value = revenue)

title: ggmemo: Add Arrows, Labels, and Change Annotations to ggplot2 Charts — ggmemo-package
code: # Label a data point annotate_callout(data, where, label, position, nudge, ...)  # Show change between two points annotate_change(data, from, to, value, format, colors, ...)  format options: "percent" (default), "absolute", "points", "both"

title: ggmemo: Add Arrows, Labels, and Change Annotations to ggplot2 Charts — ggmemo-package
text: Use ggmemo want annotate ggplot2 chart arrows, callout labels, change annotations without manually computing coordinates, formatting deltas, positioning text. Common scenarios: quarterly reports, executive dashboards, time-series narration, /comparisons.

title: ggmemo: Add Arrows, Labels, and Change Annotations to ggplot2 Charts — ggmemo-package
text: Repelling overlapping labels: use ggrepel package. NPC (normalized parent coordinates) positioning: use ggpp package. Interactive annotations: use plotly ggiraph. Theming styling: use ggthemes, hrbrthemes, bbplot.

title: ggmemo: Add Arrows, Labels, and Change Annotations to ggplot2 Charts — ggmemo-package
text: Maintainer: Lindsay Lintelman lindsay.lintelman@posit.co Authors: Lindsay Lintelman lindsay.lintelman@posit.co

title: ggmemo: Add Arrows, Labels, and Change Annotations to ggplot2 Charts — ggmemo-package
code: library(ggplot2) library(ggmemo)  # -- Complete template: narrated business chart -- # Data revenue <- data.frame(   quarter = factor(c("Q1", "Q2", "Q3", "Q4"),                    levels = c("Q1", "Q2", "Q3", "Q4")),   revenue = c(120, 145, 132, 158) )  # Annotated chart ggplot(revenue, aes(x = quarter, y = revenue)) +   geom_col(fill = "steelblue", width = 0.6) +   annotate_callout(     revenue,     where = quarter == "Q4",     label = "Record quarter",     position = "top-left"   ) +   annotate_change(     revenue,     from = quarter == "Q1",     to = quarter == "Q4",     value = revenue   ) +   labs(title = "2024 Quarterly Revenue ($K)", x = NULL, y = NULL) +   theme_minimal()

title: ggmemo 0.1.0
text: Initial release. ggmemo adds two functions annotating ggplot2 business charts without manual coordinate math.

title: ggmemo 0.1.0
text: annotate_callout() points specific data row arrow label. Supports four label positions, automatic explicit nudge, ... pass-styling. annotate_change() draws color-coded arrow two data rows labels midpoint computed delta. Supports four format options: "percent" (default), "absolute", "points" (percentage points), "". Custom colors via colors argument; label styling via .... functions use tidy evaluation row selection (, , ) — syntax dplyr::filter().

title: ggmemo 0.1.0
text: automatic nudge heuristic annotate_callout() guesses columns x y data frame structure. wide data frames many numeric columns, use nudge argument pass two-column subset data. annotate_change() similarly guesses x-axis column. heuristic prefers Date > numeric/factor > character, can pick wrong wide data. limitations tracked v0.2 (#1, #2).
